summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Cloud Types & Precipitation
summary_sections.content_html:
Clouds form when air rises, cools to its dew point, and water vapor condenses on tiny particles (condensation nuclei).

  • Cloud types by altitude:
    • High clouds (>6 km): cirrus, cirrostratus, cirrocumulus — thin, wispy, made of ice crystals
    • Middle clouds (2–6 km): altostratus, altocumulus — gray or white sheets or layers
    • Low clouds (<2 km): stratus, stratocumulus, nimbostratus — thick, gray, may produce steady precipitation
    • Vertical development: cumulus (fair weather) → cumulonimbus (thunderstorms; extend through all levels)
  • Precipitation forms: rain, snow, sleet, freezing rain, hail
  • Precipitation occurs when cloud droplets grow large enough to fall — through collision-coalescence (warm clouds) or the Bergeron process (cold clouds with ice + supercooled water).
